title: Split a god Context provider into selector-scoped contexts to stop checkout re-render storms
business_context: A B2B procurement portal wraps the entire app tree in one <AppContext> holding cart, user, feature flags, and live pricing. On the 14-line cart page, every websocket price tick (avg 6/sec during business hours) re-renders all 40k DOM nodes, causing 180ms input latency on the quantity stepper. Support logs 22 'app feels frozen' tickets/week from buyers placing 50+ line POs.
constraints: Cannot change the public hook names useApp()/useCart() consumed by 60+ components in one release
Price ticks arrive via a single shared websocket at up to 12 msg/sec
Must keep SSR hydration working under Next.js 14 app router
No new global state library may be added (security review backlog is 6 weeks)
Backward-compatible: old useApp() must keep returning the full shape during migration

before_state.description: One context object holds everything; any field change re-renders every consumer because the provider value is a fresh object each render.

issues: value is a new object literal each render, so React's Object.is check always fails for every consumer
A price tick updates prices but re-renders cart, user and flag consumers too
useCart() subscribes to the whole context, not just the cart slice
Reads whole context so re-renders on unrelated sku price changes
No memoization of the derived unit price

reasoning: Naive approach: wrap value in useMemo. Rejected because the deps array still lists prices, so a price tick busts the memo and re-renders every consumer anyway. Alternative considered: adopt Zustand for fine-grained selectors, rejected because no new dependency can clear security review in the timeline. Chosen path traces UI->state: split into four sibling contexts (UserContext, CartContext, PricesContext, FlagsContext), keep useApp() as a thin composing hook for backward compat, and have CartLine subscribe only to CartContext plus a usePrice(sku) selector backed by useSyncExternalStore over a prices store so a tick only re-renders lines holding that sku. Config: providers nest in the same order to preserve SSR; tests assert render counts via a profiler; rollout via a flag that toggles the split providers vs the legacy one.

after_state.description: Four scoped contexts plus a price store with per-sku subscriptions; only components holding a changed sku re-render.

files.purpose: Holds prices outside React and notifies only listeners subscribed to the changed sku, eliminating broad re-renders.
Per-sku selector hook; a CartLine re-renders only when its own sku's price changes.
Preserves the legacy useApp()/useCart() API so 60+ components compile unchanged during migration.
Subscribes to cart slice and a single sku price, so unrelated ticks no longer re-render this line.

migration_strategy: Land the four split providers + price store behind flag STATE_SPLIT=off, nesting them inside the legacy AppProvider so both shapes resolve
Enable STATE_SPLIT for internal employees (canary ~50 users) and watch React Profiler commit counts in RUM
Ramp to 10% then 50% of buyer tenants, comparing input-latency p95 on the cart page
Migrate hot components (CartLine, PriceBadge) to direct usePrice()/useContext(CartContext) calls while useApp() shim covers the rest
Flip STATE_SPLIT=on default; delete the legacy combined provider once render-count dashboards are flat for 7 days
testing_strategy: Fixture: a 60-line PO with 12 distinct skus and a recorded websocket tape of 200 ticks/min. Render the cart under React Test Renderer with an onRender profiler; assert that a tick on sku A increments commit count only for lines holding A (output: commit map) and that the price store's internal prices map mutated for A only (side-effect). Negative/chaos test: fire 2000 ticks across all skus in 1s and assert total commits < 3x the number of distinct on-screen skus, proving no broad re-render regression; also assert SSR snapshot getPrice matches client hydration value to prevent hydration mismatch.
what_breaks_naive_approach: useMemo on the combined value still lists prices in its dependency array, so every websocket tick produces a new memoized object and Object.is fails for all consumers — the cart, user, and flags subtrees all re-render exactly as before.

title: Replace prop-drilled useState chains with a useReducer for a 9-step insurance quote wizard
business_context: A health-insurance quoting flow drills 11 state setters through 9 wizard steps. Adding the new 'dependents' step required touching 7 files and shipped 3 regressions where Back navigation lost the smoker flag, costing the actuarial team re-quotes on ~400 applications/day. State transitions are scattered, so invalid combinations (plan=catastrophic + age<30 waiver) slip through to the rating engine.
constraints: Quote object must remain serializable to sessionStorage for refresh-resume (users abandon and return within 30 min)
Back/Forward must restore exact prior step state including derived eligibility
No transition may produce an actuarially-invalid combination that reaches the rating API
Wizard is embedded as a micro-frontend; cannot pull in Redux Toolkit (host bans it)
Must support 4 concurrent quote drafts in different tabs without cross-talk

before_state.description: Each field is its own useState in the root, passed down as setters; transitions and validation live inline in JSX handlers.

issues: 11 setters prop-drilled through every step component
No central guard, so plan='catastrophic' with age 45 reaches the API
Back navigation re-mounts steps and loses unsaved field edits
Eligibility rule (age vs plan) not enforced here, only later
Duplicated 'if (field)' guards across 9 steps

reasoning: Naive approach: lift more useState and pass an even bigger props bag. Rejected because adding a field is O(files) and Back loses state. Alternative: XState statechart, rejected because the host micro-frontend bans extra runtime deps and the team has no statechart experience to maintain it safely. Chosen: a single useReducer whose state is the serializable quote draft and whose actions ('SET_FIELD','NEXT','BACK','HYDRATE') centralize every transition; a pure validateTransition() gate runs inside the reducer so no invalid combo is ever committed. Trace: UI dispatches typed actions -> reducer validates & computes derived eligibility -> state mirrored to sessionStorage keyed by draftId for multi-tab -> rating API only ever receives reducer-validated drafts -> tests replay action logs.

after_state.description: One reducer owns the draft; steps dispatch typed actions; validity is enforced centrally and persisted per draft.

files.purpose: Single source of truth with a pure eligibility gate so invalid actuarial combinations can never be committed.
Persists each draft under a draftId key so 4 tabs keep independent, refresh-resumable state.
Steps now receive (state, dispatch) only — adding a field no longer changes any step signature.

migration_strategy: Introduce quoteReducer + useQuoteDraft behind a WIZARD_REDUCER flag, mounting it in parallel and mirroring the legacy useState values into it (read-only)
Switch one low-traffic step (Step3Plan) to dispatch while others still use setters, comparing rating-API payloads in shadow mode
Migrate remaining 8 steps to (state, dispatch); delete drilled setters per step as each is verified
Enable WIZARD_REDUCER for 20% of sessions, watching re-quote rate and invalid-combo API rejections
Default the flag on; remove legacy useState root and sessionStorage shim after one week of zero regressions
testing_strategy: Fixture: a recorded 9-step action log for a 45-year-old switching from catastrophic to standard with 2 dependents. Replay through quoteReducer and assert final Quote (output) plus that sessionStorage holds the serialized draft (side-effect). Assert that dispatching SET_FIELD plan='catastrophic' at age 45 returns the prior state unchanged (invalid combo rejected). Chaos test: interleave actions from two draftIds in the same jsdom and assert no key cross-contamination; also fire BACK 20 times past step 0 and assert step never goes negative.
what_breaks_naive_approach: With independent useStates, Back unmounts and remounts a step whose local field edits were never lifted, so the smoker flag silently resets — and because validation lives in each step's onNext, an invalid plan/age combo set in a later-edited field still reaches the rating API.

title: Introduce optimistic state for like/unlike on a social feed to kill 600ms perceived latency
business_context: A social app's feed updates like counts only after the POST round-trips through a CDN edge, averaging 600ms p75. Users double-tap thinking it failed, generating duplicate POSTs (8% of like traffic) and inflated counts that later snap back when the server reconciles. The mobile web team measures a 12% drop-off on posts where the heart doesn't fill within 300ms.
constraints: Server is the source of truth; optimistic value must reconcile on response or rollback on error
Rapid toggles (like/unlike within 200ms) must coalesce to one in-flight request per post
Must work with React 18 concurrent rendering and Suspense-wrapped feed
No duplicate POSTs may reach the backend even under double-tap
Counts must stay consistent across the same post rendered in feed and in a modal simultaneously

before_state.description: Like button waits for the network before updating; no in-flight dedupe; the same post in two places can diverge.

issues: UI updates only after network resolves -> 600ms perceived latency
No dedupe: double-tap fires two POSTs
Local state per render means the same post in a modal shows a stale count

reasoning: Naive approach: setLiked(true) immediately, then fetch. Rejected alone because it has no rollback on failure and no dedupe, so a failed request leaves a wrong filled heart and double-taps still double-POST. Alternative: React Query useMutation with onMutate optimistic update, rejected here because likes must also stay in sync across two simultaneous renderings (feed + modal) which a per-component cache key complicates, and we want a single in-flight request per post id. Chosen: a small external like-store using useSyncExternalStore so all renderings of a post share one optimistic value; an in-flight Map dedupes by post id and a generation counter discards stale responses; on error we roll back to the last server-confirmed value. Trace: UI calls store.toggle -> store applies optimistic delta + dedupes POST -> on resolve reconcile or on reject rollback -> all subscribers (feed + modal) re-read the shared snapshot -> tests assert exactly one POST per coalesced burst.

after_state.description: Shared like-store gives instant optimistic feedback, dedupes in-flight POSTs, reconciles or rolls back, and syncs all renderings.

files.purpose: Single optimistic store per post id with in-flight coalescing, generation-guarded reconciliation, and rollback.
Hook that shares one optimistic snapshot across every place the post renders (feed + modal).
Renders instant optimistic state; identical in feed and modal because both read the shared store.

migration_strategy: Ship likeStore + useLike behind OPTIMISTIC_LIKES flag, defaulting components to the legacy await-then-set path
Enable for 5% of sessions; compare duplicate-POST rate and time-to-heart-fill in RUM
Add the generation/abort coalescing and verify in shadow logs that bursts collapse to one POST
Ramp to 50%, monitoring like-count reconciliation errors and rollback frequency
Default on; remove legacy LikeButton state path after duplicate-POST rate drops below 0.5%
testing_strategy: Fixture: a stubbed api that delays 600ms and echoes the requested liked state. Assert that calling toggle() flips the snapshot synchronously (output) before the promise resolves, and that exactly one api call fires for 3 toggles within 100ms (side-effect via call count). Negative/chaos: make api reject on the 2nd call and assert the snapshot rolls back to the last confirmed value; render the same post id in two components and assert both observe the identical optimistic and reconciled snapshots, proving cross-render consistency.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A bare setLiked-then-fetch optimistic update has no in-flight registry, so a 200ms double-tap fires two POSTs that race; the second response can land first and the late first response then overwrites it, leaving the heart in the wrong state with no rollback path.

title: Move a polling-driven dashboard counter into useSyncExternalStore to share one timer across 30 widgets
business_context: An ops dashboard renders 30 KPI widgets, each running its own setInterval polling /metrics every 5s. That's 30 overlapping requests every 5s (360/min) hammering the metrics API, and tab-throttling makes intervals drift so widgets show counts from different snapshots. The SRE team gets paged on metrics-API CPU spikes that correlate exactly with dashboard concurrency.
constraints: Single source of metric truth: all widgets must read the same snapshot taken at the same instant
One poll per interval regardless of widget count (currently O(widgets))
Must pause polling when the tab is hidden and resume on focus
React 18 strict mode double-invoke must not create duplicate timers
Widgets mount/unmount as users filter; the shared poller must stop when the last widget unmounts

before_state.description: Each widget owns a timer and fetch; N widgets cause N polls and N divergent snapshots.

issues: One setInterval + fetch per widget => 30x request fan-out
Snapshots taken at different times so widgets disagree
No tab-visibility pause; throttled timers drift

reasoning: Naive approach: lift the interval to the dashboard root and pass values down as props. Rejected because it re-renders all 30 widgets on every tick even for unchanged metrics and still couples polling lifecycle to one component. Alternative: SWR with a shared key and refreshInterval, rejected because we need a single coordinated snapshot timestamp and explicit visibility-based pause that SWR's per-key revalidation complicates. Chosen: a metricsStore that owns ONE poller with reference counting via subscribe, exposes getSnapshot, and pauses on visibilitychange; widgets read individual metrics through useSyncExternalStore selecting their key. Trace: widget subscribe -> store starts the single poller on first subscriber -> visibility events gate the timer -> getSnapshot returns a frozen object so all widgets see the same instant -> last unsubscribe stops the timer -> tests assert one fetch per tick and zero timers when idle.

after_state.description: One ref-counted poller feeds a frozen snapshot; widgets select their metric and only re-render when it changes.

files.purpose: Owns a single ref-counted, visibility-aware poller and exposes one frozen snapshot for all widgets.
Selects one metric key so a widget re-renders only when its own number changes.
Reads the shared snapshot; no per-widget timer or fetch remains.

migration_strategy: Add metricsStore behind SHARED_POLLER flag; keep legacy per-widget intervals when off
Wrap dashboard in MetricsStoreProvider and migrate 3 pilot widgets to useMetric, comparing /metrics request volume in the gateway
Verify strict-mode double subscribe does not double the poller (ref count stays at 1)
Migrate all widgets; assert request rate drops from O(widgets) to one per interval
Remove legacy KpiWidget interval code after API CPU graphs confirm the drop for 48h
testing_strategy: Fixture: a fake api returning incrementing snapshots and fake timers. Subscribe 30 widgets, advance 5s, assert exactly one api call fired (side-effect) and all 30 read the same snapshot value (output). Assert unsubscribing all widgets clears the timer (no pending interval). Chaos test: dispatch visibilitychange to hidden mid-interval and advance 30s, asserting zero polls occur while hidden, then resume on visible and confirm polling restarts; also mount/unmount widgets rapidly under strict mode and assert refs never exceeds the live widget count.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Lifting one interval to the root still re-renders all 30 widgets each tick because the snapshot object identity changes, and per-widget intervals (the original) keep firing N concurrent /metrics requests that desynchronize whenever the browser throttles background timers.

title: Derive cart totals with a memoized selector layer instead of duplicated total math in 12 components
business_context: An e-commerce cart computes subtotal, tax, shipping, and discounts inline in 12 different components (mini-cart, drawer, checkout summary, sticky footer...). A promo-code bug shipped because the discount formula was fixed in 9 places but missed in 3, charging 1,100 customers the wrong total over a weekend and triggering chargebacks. Each component recomputes O(lines) math on every keystroke in the quantity field.
constraints: Total math must live in exactly one place; all 12 surfaces must agree to the cent
Recompute only when inputs (lines, promo, address) change, not on every render
Tax rules vary by ship-to state and must stay pluggable
Must support 500-line wholesale carts without jank (<16ms recompute)
Backward-compatible: components currently import a useCart() hook that returns raw lines

before_state.description: Each component imports raw lines and recomputes totals locally; formulas drift and recompute every render.

issues: Total math duplicated across 12 components and drifts
Discount formula hardcoded 0.1 in some files, 0.15 in others after a bad fix
Recomputes on every render, even when only an unrelated field changed

reasoning: Naive approach: extract a totals() function and call it in each component. Rejected because each component still calls it every render with no memoization, so a 500-line cart recomputes 12x per keystroke. Alternative: store precomputed totals in context state and recompute in an effect on every change, rejected because effects run after paint causing a flash of stale totals and risk of infinite loops. Chosen: a pure selectCartTotals(state) pure function plus a useCartTotals() hook that memoizes via useMemo keyed on the exact inputs (lines ref, promo, state), with pluggable tax strategy injected. Trace: UI calls useCartTotals -> single memoized selector computes once per input change -> all 12 components read identical memoized object -> tax strategy injected via context for state rules -> tests assert one computation per input change and cent-equality across surfaces.

after_state.description: One pure selector computes all totals; a memoized hook shares the result so every surface agrees and recompute is input-gated.

files.purpose: The single canonical totals formula in cents; any pricing fix happens here once.
Memoizes the canonical totals so a 500-line cart recomputes once per real input change, not per render.
Reads the shared memoized total; contains zero pricing math so it can never drift.

migration_strategy: Land selectTotals + useCartTotals behind CART_SELECTORS flag with a unit-test snapshot pinning current cent values
Migrate CheckoutSummary first and diff its total against the legacy inline math in shadow logging for 24h
Migrate the remaining 11 surfaces one per deploy, each verified cent-equal to the selector
Enable CART_SELECTORS by default once all surfaces match
Delete inline total math from all components and add a lint rule banning price*qty outside selectTotals
testing_strategy: Fixture: a 500-line cart with a promo and a CA ship-to address. Assert selectTotals returns exact cents (output) and that useCartTotals recomputes exactly once when only qty of one line changes via a render-counted memo probe (side-effect). Property test: feed 10k random carts and assert total === subtotal - discount + tax + ship to the cent. Negative test: swap the tax strategy to a state with 0% and assert tax === 0 and total still balances; assert two different surfaces given the same input return strictly equal totals objects.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A plain extracted totals() called in each component recomputes O(lines) on every render — a 500-line cart with 12 mounted surfaces does 6,000 multiplications per keystroke — and an effect-based precompute paints stale totals for one frame before correcting, which checkout users see as a flickering price.

title: Split a Redux-style mega store into a useReducer + Context per bounded context for a logistics dispatch board
business_context: A last-mile dispatch board keeps drivers, routes, orders, and map viewport in one giant reducer. A viewport pan (fired ~30x/sec while dragging the map) re-runs the entire reducer and re-renders the 800-row order table, dropping the drag to 11fps. Dispatchers managing 300 active drivers report the board 'locks up' during route reassignment, and 4 wrong-driver assignments happened last month from laggy clicks.
constraints: Map viewport changes must not re-render the order table or driver list
Drag at 30 events/sec must hold 60fps on a 300-driver board
Reducer state must remain time-travel debuggable for incident replay
Order assignments are authoritative and must never be lost during a re-render storm
Cannot rewrite the websocket ingestion layer that feeds all four domains

before_state.description: One reducer + one context; every domain action re-renders every consumer.

issues: A PAN action returns a new state object so all consumers (table, list, map) re-render
800-row order table re-renders on every viewport pan
value={{state,dispatch}} is a new object each render
Subscribes to the whole board state, so a viewport pan re-renders 800 rows
No row-level memoization

reasoning: Naive approach: wrap the table in React.memo. Rejected because it still reads the whole context value which changes identity on every PAN, so memo never helps. Alternative: introduce Redux Toolkit with reselect selectors, rejected because the team wants to keep the lightweight useReducer model and avoid a store-library migration mid-quarter. Chosen: split into four reducer+context pairs (ViewportContext, OrdersContext, DriversContext, RoutesContext) so a PAN dispatch only touches ViewportContext; the order table subscribes solely to OrdersContext. Trace: drag UI -> dispatch PAN to ViewportContext only -> map re-renders, table/list untouched -> order assignments live in OrdersContext untouched by pans -> websocket ingestion fans out to the right context's dispatch -> tests assert table render count stays flat during a pan storm.

after_state.description: Four scoped reducer/context pairs; pans isolate to the viewport context and the order table only re-renders on order changes.

files.purpose: Factory yields one reducer+context per bounded context with a memoized value so unrelated domains never share render churn.
Routes each websocket message to exactly one domain's dispatch, keeping ingestion unchanged but scoped.
Subscribes only to OrdersContext so viewport pans no longer re-render any of the 800 rows.

migration_strategy: Introduce the four providers nested inside the legacy BoardProvider behind BOARD_SPLIT flag, mirroring legacy state read-only
Route PAN dispatches to ViewportContext for an internal canary and profile the order table commit count during a drag
Move ASSIGN/DRIVER_PING ingestion to scoped dispatches, validating no assignment is dropped via event-log diff
Ramp to all dispatchers; confirm 60fps drag on a 300-driver board in RUM
Delete the monolithic boardReducer and BoardProvider after a week of stable assignment integrity
testing_strategy: Fixture: a 800-order, 300-driver board plus a recorded drag tape of 300 PAN actions. Render under a profiler and assert OrderTable commit count stays at its initial value through the pan storm (side-effect), while the map's viewport value updates (output). Assert an ASSIGN dispatched mid-pan still lands in OrdersContext state. Chaos test: interleave 1000 PANs with 50 ASSIGN and 200 DRIVER_PING in random order and assert every assignment is present in final orders state and zero table re-renders were caused by pans.
what_breaks_naive_approach: React.memo on the table compares props, but the table reads context whose value object identity changes on every PAN, so memo's prop check is irrelevant and all 800 rows re-render on each of the 30 pan events per second.

title: Replace ad-hoc loading booleans with a status state machine reducer for a file-upload pipeline
business_context: A medical imaging portal tracks uploads with isUploading, isError, isDone, and isRetrying booleans. Impossible combos (isDone && isError both true) render a green check over a red banner, confusing radiologists who then re-upload 70MB DICOM files unnecessarily — 240 duplicate uploads/day at $0.02/GB egress. Retry logic reads stale booleans and sometimes retries a finished upload, corrupting the study by appending a second copy.
constraints: State must be a single discriminated status, never multiple booleans
Retry must be impossible from a terminal 'done' state
Must survive a page refresh mid-upload (resumable upload sessions)
70MB files; UI must reflect progress without re-rendering the whole upload list
HIPAA: no PHI in client logs during state transitions

before_state.description: Four independent booleans encode status; impossible combinations are representable and retries read stale flags.

issues: isDone and isError can both be true, rendering check + error together
Retry calls start() even from a done state, duplicating the upload
Booleans don't survive refresh, so a mid-upload reload shows nothing

reasoning: Naive approach: add guards like if(!isDone) before retry. Rejected because guard sprawl across booleans is exactly what already failed; the impossible states remain representable. Alternative: adopt XState, rejected because the team needs resumable persistence keyed by upload session id and prefers a tiny serializable reducer they can snapshot to IndexedDB. Chosen: a discriminated-union status reducer ('idle'|'uploading'|'error'|'done') where transitions are total functions and 'done' has no RETRY transition, making double-upload unrepresentable; status persists to IndexedDB for resume. Trace: UI dispatches START/PROGRESS/SUCCESS/FAIL/RETRY -> reducer only allows valid transitions -> status snapshot saved per session id -> refresh rehydrates the exact status -> tests assert no transition out of 'done' except reset.

after_state.description: A single status union reducer makes impossible combos unrepresentable and persists per-session for resume.

files.purpose: Total transition function where 'done' accepts only RESET, so a finished upload can never be retried into a duplicate.
Drives the machine and persists each status to IndexedDB per session id for refresh-resume.
Renders exactly one branch per status, so the check-over-error visual contradiction is structurally impossible.

migration_strategy: Add uploadMachine + useUpload behind UPLOAD_FSM flag while leaving boolean components live
Migrate one upload surface; shadow-log every transition and assert no done->uploading transition occurs
Wire IndexedDB persistence and verify mid-upload refresh resumes the correct status
Ramp to all users; watch duplicate-upload egress metric fall
Remove boolean state and the old retry path once duplicate uploads drop near zero
testing_strategy: Fixture: a recorded event log driving idle->uploading->done. Assert final status (output) and that saveStatus wrote a 'done' snapshot to a fake IndexedDB (side-effect). Assert dispatching RETRY on a 'done' status returns the same 'done' status (no duplicate upload). Chaos test: replay a random sequence of 500 events and assert the status tag is always one of the four valid tags and never holds contradictory fields; simulate a refresh by reloading from the fake store mid-uploading and assert progress resumes from the persisted value.
what_breaks_naive_approach: With four booleans, a FAIL after a SUCCESS sets isError true while isDone stays true, so the UI shows both a check and an error and the retry handler — reading the still-false-looking guards — re-runs the upload, appending a duplicate copy to the patient study.

title: Adopt useSyncExternalStore to subscribe a chart to a Redux store slice without tearing under concurrent rendering
business_context: A trading terminal renders a depth chart from a Redux store fed by a 50 msg/sec price feed. After upgrading to React 18 with startTransition for the order grid, the chart began 'tearing' — showing bid/ask from two different store versions in the same frame during a concurrent render, which traders flagged as wrong spreads and stopped trusting the screen.
constraints: No tearing: every committed render must reflect a single consistent store snapshot
50 updates/sec must not block the order-grid transition
Must keep using the existing Redux store (firm standard)
Selector must support equality to avoid re-render on unchanged spread
Works under React 18 concurrent features (startTransition, Suspense)

before_state.description: A useState+subscribe pattern copies the slice into local state, which can read a stale snapshot mid-concurrent-render and tear.

issues: setState-in-subscribe can render with a snapshot inconsistent with siblings
Effect subscribes after paint, missing updates between render and effect
No equality check; spread re-renders on every tick

reasoning: Naive approach: keep the useState+subscribe selector. Rejected because React 18 may render the chart with a torn snapshot relative to the order grid in the same concurrent pass. Alternative: move the slice into React state via a Redux Toolkit listener middleware that dispatches to component state, rejected as it duplicates the source of truth and adds latency. Chosen: useSyncExternalStore, the API designed to prevent tearing, with a memoized selector and a custom isEqual to skip unchanged spreads. Trace: chart calls useSyncExternalStore(store.subscribe, () => selector(store.getState())) -> React guarantees a consistent snapshot per commit -> equality skips no-op spreads -> works alongside startTransition on the grid -> tests assert no two child reads diverge in one render.

after_state.description: useSyncExternalStore binds the chart to the Redux snapshot with tear-free guarantees and selector equality.

files.purpose: Tear-free Redux subscription with memoized equality so unchanged spreads do not re-render.
Reads a single consistent bid/ask snapshot per commit, eliminating cross-frame tearing.
Shallow equality so spread re-renders only when bid or ask actually changes.

migration_strategy: Add useReduxSelector and migrate the depth chart behind USS_CHART flag, keeping useSlice elsewhere
Run a tearing detector in canary that flags any frame where chart bid/ask differs from grid snapshot version
Ramp to all terminals while monitoring the tearing counter at zero
Migrate remaining tick-driven components to useReduxSelector
Delete useSlice and add a lint ban on setState-in-store-subscribe patterns
testing_strategy: Fixture: a Redux store driven by a 50-tick tape and a sibling component reading the same store version counter. Render under concurrent mode with startTransition on the sibling; assert chart and sibling always read the same version per commit (output) and that no re-render occurs on a tick that leaves bid/ask unchanged (side-effect via render counter). Chaos test: dispatch 5,000 ticks while continuously triggering low-priority transitions and assert the tearing counter stays at 0; force a stale snapshot by mutating state between render and commit and assert useSyncExternalStore re-renders to converge.
what_breaks_naive_approach: The useState+subscribe selector updates via setState which React 18 can schedule across a concurrent render boundary, so the chart commits a price snapshot from version N while the order grid in the same pass shows version N+1 — a visible torn spread.

title: Centralize form state into a reducer with field-level validation for a 60-field KYC onboarding
business_context: A fintech KYC form has 60 fields across 5 sections, each a separate useState with onBlur validation copied inline. Cross-field rules (SSN required only if country==US, tax-id format depends on entity type) are scattered, so 9% of submissions fail server validation and bounce back, adding a day to account opening. The submit button's enabled state is computed from a 40-term boolean expression that's wrong in two edge cases.
constraints: Validation rules must be declarative and live in one schema
Cross-field rules must re-run when their dependencies change
Submit-enabled must be a single derived value, never a hand-written boolean
Form state must serialize for 'save and continue later' (regulatory 7-day window)
Must support partial server-side validation merge without losing local edits

before_state.description: 60 useStates with inline onBlur validators and a giant hand-written submit-enabled expression.

issues: 60 useStates make cross-field rules read stale values
canSubmit is a 40-term hand-written boolean wrong in 2 cases
Validators duplicated per field with inconsistent regex

reasoning: Naive approach: add more useState and more inline validators. Rejected because cross-field validators read stale closure values and submit-enabled drifts. Alternative: pull in React Hook Form + Yup, rejected because the firm requires the form state to be a plain serializable object for the regulatory save-and-resume vault and wants no resolver black box. Chosen: a single reducer holding { values, errors, touched } with a declarative schema of field validators (including cross-field dependencies); a pure selectCanSubmit derives the button state. Trace: input dispatches SET_FIELD -> reducer updates values then re-runs validators whose deps changed -> selectCanSubmit derives enabled from errors -> whole state serializes to the vault -> server validation merges via MERGE_ERRORS without clobbering values -> tests assert dependency-driven revalidation.

after_state.description: One reducer with a declarative schema runs dependency-aware validation and derives submit-enabled.

files.purpose: Declarative cross-field rules; one place to read what must revalidate when a field changes.
Re-runs only the changed field and its dependents; derives submit-enabled from errors so it can't drift.
Each field dispatches one action; submit-enabled is derived, never hand-written.

migration_strategy: Land schema + formReducer behind KYC_REDUCER; mirror legacy useState into it read-only and compare canSubmit values
Migrate one section (identity) to dispatch; shadow-compare server validation bounce rate
Wire MERGE_ERRORS so server-side failures attach to fields without losing local edits
Migrate all 5 sections; enable the flag for 25% and watch the 9% bounce rate
Delete per-field useStates and the 40-term boolean after bounce rate stabilizes
testing_strategy: Fixture: a US individual with an invalid 8-digit SSN. Dispatch SET_FIELD country='US' then ssn='12345678' and assert errors.ssn is set (output) and selectCanSubmit is false. Assert changing country to 'CA' clears the SSN error because the cross-field rule re-runs (side-effect on errors map). Negative test: MERGE_ERRORS from server attaches a taxId error and assert local values untouched; property test: random field orders produce the same final errors map regardless of edit sequence.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Independent useStates capture stale values in each validator closure, so setting country=US after SSN was entered never re-validates SSN, and the hand-written 40-term canSubmit expression silently allows two invalid combinations through to the server.

title: Add optimistic reordering with rollback for a Kanban board backed by a Postgres rank column
business_context: A B2B project tool lets users drag cards between columns. Each drop awaits a PATCH that recomputes a fractional rank in Postgres; during the ~400ms await the card snaps back to its origin then jumps to the new spot, which 30% of users misread as a failed drop and re-drag, creating rank-collision retries. Two users dragging in the same column concurrently sometimes overwrite each other's order.
constraints: Drop must show the new position instantly, before the PATCH resolves
On PATCH failure the card must animate back to its exact prior index
Concurrent reorders by two users must converge (last-write-wins on rank, no lost cards)
Must not refetch the whole board on every drop (board has 2,000 cards)
Optimistic state must reconcile with server-assigned fractional rank

before_state.description: Drop awaits the server then sets state, so the card visibly snaps back; concurrent edits clobber.

issues: UI updates only after PATCH + full board refetch (~400ms+)
Refetches all 2,000 cards on every drop
No rollback; failure leaves card wherever the refetch put it
No reconciliation of concurrent reorders

reasoning: Naive approach: optimistically splice the card then refetch on success. Rejected because the full-board refetch is the latency source and concurrent drops still race. Alternative: server push via websocket reconciling every client, rejected as over-scoped for this milestone and the backend channel isn't ready. Chosen: an optimistic reducer that applies the move locally with a temporary fractional rank, sends the PATCH, and on success patches in the server's authoritative rank for just that card; on failure it reverts to the snapshotted prior order. Concurrency converges because each card's rank is replaced independently by the server value. Trace: drop -> dispatch MOVE_OPTIMISTIC with interpolated rank -> PATCH -> RECONCILE one card's rank or REVERT on error -> only the moved card updates, no refetch -> tests assert rollback index equals origin.

after_state.description: An optimistic reducer moves the card instantly with an interpolated rank and reconciles or reverts a single card.

files.purpose: Moves one card optimistically with an interpolated rank; reconciles or reverts exactly that card without touching others.
Orchestrates optimistic move, server reconcile of the authoritative rank, or revert on failure — no board refetch.
Triggers an instant optimistic reorder; only the dragged card changes, eliminating the snap-back.

migration_strategy: Ship useReorder behind OPTIMISTIC_DND flag; default to the await-then-refetch path
Enable for an internal project; measure snap-back occurrences and re-drag rate
Verify REVERT restores the exact prior index by diffing snapshot vs reverted order in logs
Ramp to all tenants; confirm board PATCH p95 unchanged and refetch volume drops to zero
Remove the full-board refetch path after re-drag rate falls below 2%
testing_strategy: Fixture: a 2,000-card board; move card X from rank 10 to between 20 and 21. Assert the optimistic rank is the midpoint (output) and that no /api/board fetch fired (side-effect via fetch spy). Make the PATCH reject and assert REVERT restores X to its original index exactly. Chaos test: two concurrent move() calls on the same column with stubbed server ranks; assert both cards end at their server-assigned ranks with no card lost and the optimistic temporaries are fully replaced.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Optimistic splice + full refetch still blocks the visible correction on a 2,000-card round-trip, and because both racing drops trigger independent full refetches, the slower response overwrites the faster one's ordering — a card silently jumps back.

title: Split theme + auth + i18n out of a single Context to stop a locale switch from re-rendering authed routes
business_context: A SaaS admin console holds theme, current user, and locale in one ConfigContext. Switching language (a common QA action, ~2,000/day in support sessions) re-renders the entire authed app tree including a 1,500-row audit table, freezing the UI for ~700ms. The same re-render re-runs an expensive permission computation tied to the user object, even though only locale changed.
constraints: Locale change must not re-render auth-gated subtrees or re-run permission checks
Theme toggle must not re-render data tables
Provider order must keep auth available to i18n (translated role names)
No regression to the existing useConfig() consumers (90+ call sites)
SSR theme must match client to avoid flash of wrong theme

before_state.description: One ConfigContext bundles theme/user/locale; any change re-renders all consumers and re-runs permission memo.

issues: value is a fresh object so a locale change re-renders theme + auth consumers
Permission memo keyed on the whole config recomputes on locale change
1,500-row table consumes useConfig() and re-renders on theme toggles

reasoning: Naive approach: memo the value with useMemo. Rejected because deps still include locale, so a locale change busts the memo and re-renders auth consumers anyway. Alternative: a single store with selectors via useSyncExternalStore, rejected as overkill since these three concerns rarely change and simple context splitting is cheaper to maintain. Chosen: three independent contexts (ThemeContext, AuthContext, LocaleContext); the permission memo moves into AuthContext keyed only on user; useConfig() becomes a backward-compat composer. Trace: locale toggle -> LocaleProvider state changes -> only translated text re-renders -> AuthContext untouched so permission memo holds -> theme isolated from tables -> SSR theme passed as initial prop -> tests assert audit table doesn't re-render on locale change.

after_state.description: Three scoped providers; permission memo lives in auth, so locale and theme changes no longer cascade.

files.purpose: Permission computation depends only on user, so a locale change cannot trigger it.
Isolated locale state so only translated text re-renders on a language switch.
Backward-compat composer so the 90+ existing useConfig() call sites compile unchanged.
Nests providers so auth is available inside i18n for translated role names and SSR theme is seeded.

migration_strategy: Mount the three providers nested inside legacy ConfigProvider behind CONFIG_SPLIT, useConfig() reads new ones
Migrate the audit table to useLocale()/useTheme() directly and profile re-renders on a locale switch
Move computePermissions into AuthContext; verify it does not run on locale change in canary
Ramp the flag to 100%; confirm locale-switch frame time drops below 100ms
Delete the legacy ConfigContext after dashboards confirm no auth re-render on theme/locale changes
testing_strategy: Fixture: an authed console with a 1,500-row audit table and a recorded locale switch. Render under a profiler; assert the audit table commit count is unchanged after a locale change (side-effect) while translated headers update (output). Assert computePermissions is called once at mount and not again on a locale toggle (spy). Chaos test: toggle locale and theme 200 times rapidly and assert permission computation count stays at 1; verify SSR-provided theme equals the client theme on hydration to avoid a flash.
what_breaks_naive_approach: useMemo on the bundled value lists locale in its deps, so a language switch produces a new value object, fails Object.is for every consumer, re-renders the 1,500-row table, and re-runs the user-keyed permission computation despite the user being unchanged.

title: Replace context-stored server cache with React Query while keeping a thin Context for derived UI state
business_context: An e-commerce PDP stores fetched product, reviews, and inventory in a ProductContext via useEffect + useState. The same product refetches on every navigation because the context resets, causing 3 redundant API calls per product view (1.2M extra calls/day) and a loading spinner flash even for cached products. Stale inventory shows 'in stock' for items sold out 10 minutes ago because there's no revalidation.
constraints: Server data must be cached and revalidated, not re-fetched on every mount
Inventory must revalidate on window focus (stale-while-revalidate)
Derived UI-only state (selected variant, gallery index) must stay local, not in server cache
No waterfall: product, reviews, inventory fetch in parallel
Backward-compatible useProduct() hook shape for 25 components

before_state.description: Server data lives in context state fetched by effects; it resets per navigation and never revalidates.

issues: Refetches on every navigation; no cache
Reviews waits on product -> waterfall
No focus revalidation -> stale inventory
Mixes server cache with what should be UI state

reasoning: Naive approach: cache responses in a module-level object keyed by id. Rejected because it reimplements a query cache poorly with no revalidation, dedupe, or focus handling. Alternative: keep context but add manual stale timers, rejected as fragile and duplicative of established tooling. Chosen: React Query (TanStack) owns server cache (product/reviews/inventory) with parallel queries, staleTime, and refetchOnWindowFocus for inventory; a thin ProductUIContext keeps only UI-derived state (selected variant, gallery index). useProduct() composes query results to preserve the old shape. Trace: PDP mounts -> three parallel queries hit the QueryClient cache (no refetch if fresh) -> inventory revalidates on focus -> UI state stays in a tiny context -> useProduct() returns the legacy shape -> tests assert single fetch per cache window.

after_state.description: React Query owns cached, revalidating server data; a thin context holds only UI-derived state.

files.purpose: Parallel cached queries; inventory revalidates on focus so sold-out items refresh.
Holds only client-derived UI state, cleanly separated from cached server data.
Composes the three parallel queries into the legacy shape so 25 components compile unchanged.

migration_strategy: Add QueryClientProvider and queries behind RQ_PDP flag; keep ProductProvider live but read from queries when flagged
Migrate useProduct() to compose queries; verify parallel fetch (no waterfall) in network panel for canary
Enable focus revalidation for inventory and confirm sold-out items refresh on tab focus
Ramp to 100%; watch redundant product-call volume drop in the gateway
Delete ProductProvider effects and keep only ProductUIContext for UI state
testing_strategy: Fixture: navigate to the same product twice within the staleTime window using a mocked QueryClient. Assert the product queryFn runs once (side-effect via fetch spy) and the second navigation returns cached data with isLoading false (output, no spinner). Assert reviews and inventory fetch in parallel (both started before product resolves). Chaos test: dispatch a window focus event after staleTime and assert only inventory refetches; mark the inventory endpoint to fail and assert the cached product still renders without unmounting.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A module-level cache object has no request dedupe or revalidation, so two PDP mounts in flight both fire, and stale inventory never refreshes — the page keeps showing 'in stock' because nothing invalidates the cached entry on focus.

title: Move undo/redo from component snapshots to a reducer with a command history for a vector editor
business_context: A design tool snapshots the whole canvas JSON into a useState array for undo/redo. A 2,000-shape document makes each snapshot ~4MB; 50 edits = 200MB heap and the tab OOMs on Chrome after ~20 minutes of editing. Undo is also O(document) because it deep-clones, so Ctrl+Z stutters for 300ms on large files.
constraints: Undo/redo must store deltas (commands), not full snapshots
Memory must stay bounded regardless of edit count (cap history depth)
Undo/redo must be O(delta), not O(document)
Must coalesce rapid edits (dragging a handle = one undo step)
Document state must stay serializable for autosave

before_state.description: Full-document snapshots per edit blow up memory and make undo O(document).

issues: structuredClone of a 4MB doc per edit -> OOM after ~50 edits
Undo restores a full snapshot, O(document)
No coalescing: a drag of 60 frames creates 60 history entries

reasoning: Naive approach: cap the snapshot array length. Rejected because each retained snapshot is still 4MB and undo is still O(document). Alternative: immer patches captured automatically, rejected here because the team wants explicit invertible commands for analytics and server replay, and immer's patch granularity over deep nested shapes is coarse. Chosen: a reducer over a command list where each command carries do()/undo() deltas (e.g., move shape by dx,dy); history stores commands not snapshots, capped to N; a coalesce key merges consecutive same-shape drags into one command. Trace: drag -> dispatch APPLY(command) -> reducer applies delta to doc and pushes the inverse -> undo pops and applies inverse -> autosave serializes the small doc -> tests assert memory bounded and drag = one undo step.

after_state.description: A command-history reducer stores invertible deltas, bounding memory and making undo O(delta).

files.purpose: Invertible delta commands so a move stores only dx/dy, not a 4MB snapshot.
History of capped commands; undo/redo apply one delta and coalescing merges drag frames into a single step.
Dispatches commands; memory is bounded and undo is one delta regardless of document size.

migration_strategy: Land command reducer behind COMMAND_HISTORY flag; keep snapshot undo for documents not yet migrated
Migrate move/resize/rotate to commands; verify undo output matches the old snapshot-based result on a fixture doc
Enable coalescing and confirm a drag produces exactly one undo step in canary
Cap history at 200 and ramp to all users; monitor tab heap over a 30-min session
Remove snapshot history once heap stays flat and undo latency drops below 16ms
testing_strategy: Fixture: a 2,000-shape doc; apply 60 move commands with the same shape key (a simulated drag). Assert the doc reflects the net move (output) and that past.length increased by exactly 1 (side-effect: coalesced). Apply UNDO and assert it inverts in O(1) shape touch. Memory test: apply 10,000 distinct commands and assert past.length never exceeds the 200 cap. Chaos test: random interleaving of APPLY/UNDO/REDO 5,000 times and assert doc equals replaying past commands from initial — history stays consistent.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Capping the snapshot array still keeps each retained 4MB snapshot in memory and undo deep-clones the whole document, so on a 2,000-shape file Ctrl+Z stalls for 300ms and a long session OOMs the tab regardless of the cap.

title: Lift filter/sort/pagination state into a URL-synced reducer so shared links restore the exact data view
business_context: An analytics product keeps table filters, sort, and page in component useState. Users share a dashboard URL with a teammate but the link opens the default view because filter state never serializes to the URL, generating ~150 'why does my link look different' support threads/month. Back button doesn't restore the prior filter set either, so users lose a 6-facet filter on accidental navigation.
constraints: Filter/sort/page state must serialize to and restore from the URL query string
Back/forward must restore exact prior view (history integration)
Invalid URL params must coerce to safe defaults, never crash the table
Must debounce URL writes during rapid filter typing (no history spam)
Backward-compatible: the table component API stays the same

before_state.description: Filters live in useState; nothing serializes to the URL, so shared links and back-button lose state.

issues: No URL serialization -> shared links open default view
Back button doesn't restore prior filters
Refresh resets to page 1 / no filters

reasoning: Naive approach: write filters to the URL in a useEffect on every change. Rejected because it spams history on each keystroke and creating new history entries breaks back navigation. Alternative: store state in localStorage, rejected because it isn't shareable via a link and diverges across users. Chosen: a reducer holds the view state; a useUrlState hook serializes it to the query string with replaceState (debounced) for in-session edits and pushState for navigable changes, and parses+coerces params on load. Trace: filter change -> dispatch SET_FILTER -> reducer updates view -> debounced serialize to URL via replaceState -> on popstate, parse URL and dispatch HYDRATE -> query layer reads view -> tests assert link restores exact view and invalid params coerce.

after_state.description: A view reducer synced to the URL via debounced replace/push so links and back-button restore the exact view.

files.purpose: Pure view transitions including auto-reset to page 1 on filter change; HYDRATE restores from URL.
Serializes/parses the view to query params and coerces invalid params to safe defaults.
Debounced URL writes and popstate hydration so back/forward and shared links restore the exact view.

migration_strategy: Add useUrlView behind URL_VIEW flag; mount it parallel to useState and compare derived query keys
Migrate DataTable to dispatch and read view; verify a copied URL opens the same filtered view in canary
Add popstate hydration and confirm back button restores a 6-facet filter set
Ramp to 100%; watch the 'different link' support thread volume
Remove legacy useState filters once shareable links are verified across browsers
testing_strategy: Fixture: apply 6 filters, a sort, and page 3, then read location.search. Assert the URL contains all facets (output) and that only one replaceState fired after the debounce window despite 6 rapid changes (side-effect via spy). Load a hand-crafted URL with page=abc and assert paramsToView coerces page to 1 (no crash). Chaos test: dispatch popstate with a malformed query string and assert the table renders defaults; simulate back navigation and assert HYDRATE restores the prior filter set exactly.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Writing filters to the URL via pushState on every keystroke creates a history entry per character, so the back button walks through partial filter strings instead of returning to the previous view, and the rapid writes flood the history stack.

title: Build a useSyncExternalStore adapter over a Web Worker game state for a 10k-entity simulation
business_context: A browser city-builder runs its simulation in a Web Worker but mirrors entity state into React via postMessage->setState, dispatching 60 setState calls/sec for 10,000 entities. React re-renders the entire entity layer each frame, dropping to 18fps. The minimap and the inspector panel both keep their own copies of the same entity data, which drift when messages arrive out of order.
constraints: A single source of truth mirrored from the worker, read by all panels
60Hz worker updates must not re-render unchanged panels
Inspector and minimap must read the same entity snapshot (no drift)
Out-of-order worker messages must be applied by version, newest wins
Must not block the main thread parsing 10k entities each frame

before_state.description: Worker messages call setState directly; multiple components keep divergent copies and re-render every frame.

issues: setState 60x/sec with the full 10k map re-renders all consumers
Each component instance holds its own copy -> drift
No versioning -> out-of-order messages clobber newer data

reasoning: Naive approach: lift useEntities to the root and pass entities down. Rejected because every frame still produces a new map and re-renders all panels. Alternative: Redux with throttled dispatch, rejected because dispatching 10k-entity actions at 60Hz and running reducers/selectors on the main thread is the bottleneck we're trying to remove. Chosen: an external entityStore updated directly by worker messages (outside React), versioned so stale messages drop; panels read via useSyncExternalStore with narrow selectors (minimap reads positions, inspector reads one entity). Trace: worker -> store.applyFrame(version, entities) outside React -> store notifies -> selectors read only their slice -> unchanged selectors skip re-render via equality -> single snapshot shared, no drift -> tests assert version ordering and per-panel render isolation.

after_state.description: An external versioned store fed by the worker; panels select narrow slices via useSyncExternalStore.

files.purpose: Single versioned store updated outside React; stale frames are dropped so order doesn't cause drift.
Narrow selectors: inspector subscribes to one entity, minimap to the version, so panels render independently.
Feeds worker frames straight into the store without touching React state, avoiding per-frame setState.

migration_strategy: Introduce entityStore + wireWorker behind WORKER_STORE flag; keep useEntities setState path live
Migrate the inspector to useEntity(id) and confirm it re-renders only when its entity changes
Migrate the minimap to usePositions/version selector; profile fps during a 10k-entity frame storm
Ramp to all sessions; verify no panel drift by asserting both panels read the same store version
Remove the setState mirror once fps holds at 60 and drift reports stop
testing_strategy: Fixture: a recorded sequence of worker frames including one out-of-order (version 5 arriving after version 6). Assert applyFrame ignores the stale frame so getVersion stays 6 (output). Render inspector for entity A and minimap; advance 60 frames where only entity B moves and assert the inspector's render count is unchanged (side-effect). Chaos test: deliver 600 frames with 5% shuffled order and assert final state equals the highest-version frame and inspector/minimap read identical entity A data.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Lifting useEntities to the root still calls setState with a fresh 10k-entity object every frame, so React diffs the entire entity layer 60 times per second; and because each panel kept its own copy, an out-of-order message updates one copy but not the other, so the minimap and inspector show different positions.

title: Decompose a Zustand monolith store into slices with shallow selectors to stop notification re-renders
business_context: A devtools dashboard uses one Zustand store for builds, logs, notifications, and user settings. A notification toast (fires ~40x during a busy CI run) updates the store and, because components subscribe without selectors, re-renders the 5,000-line log viewer each time, causing visible scroll jank. The store file is 1,200 lines and every feature edit risks unrelated regressions.
constraints: Notification updates must not re-render the log viewer or build list
Selectors must use shallow equality to avoid object-identity re-renders
Slices must be independently testable and < 200 lines each
Store API (useStore) must stay stable for 70 call sites
Persisted settings slice must rehydrate without blocking first paint

before_state.description: One giant store; components subscribe to the whole state so any slice change re-renders everything.

issues: 1,200-line store mixes 4 domains
Components call useStore() with no selector -> subscribe to all state
A notification push re-renders the log viewer
Subscribes to whole store, re-renders on notification pushes
No selector or equality function

reasoning: Naive approach: add a selector useStore(s => s.logs). Rejected as a half-measure: it helps the log viewer but the monolith remains unmaintainable and other components still over-subscribe. Alternative: migrate to Redux Toolkit, rejected because Zustand is the team standard and a full rewrite isn't justified. Chosen: split the store into composable slices (buildsSlice, logsSlice, notificationsSlice, settingsSlice) combined into one store; every component reads via a typed selector with shallow equality; the settings slice uses persist with skipHydration to avoid blocking paint. Trace: notification action -> updates only notifications slice -> components with logs selector keep referential equality -> shallow selectors prevent re-render -> persisted settings rehydrate async -> tests assert log viewer render count flat during a toast storm.

after_state.description: Composable slices with shallow selectors; a notification push touches only its slice.

files.purpose: Each domain is an isolated, independently testable slice under 200 lines.
Combines slices into one store with the same useStore API; only settings persist and hydration is deferred.
Selects only the logs slice with shallow equality, so notification pushes no longer re-render it.

migration_strategy: Refactor the store into slices behind the same useStore export; behavior identical, no flag needed for the split
Add shallow selectors to the 10 hottest components (log viewer, build list) and profile a notification storm
Enable persist with skipHydration and call rehydrate() after first paint; verify no hydration blocking
Audit remaining call sites with a lint rule flagging selector-less useStore() usage
Migrate all 70 call sites to selectors; confirm log viewer render count stays flat during CI runs
testing_strategy: Fixture: a store with 5,000 logs; render LogViewer with the shallow logs selector and fire 40 addNotification calls. Assert LogViewer render count stays at 1 (side-effect via render counter) and notifications array grows to 40 (output). Assert the persisted snapshot contains only settings, not logs (partialize). Chaos test: interleave 1,000 appendLog and addNotification calls and assert each slice's state is independent and the build list never re-rendered due to log changes when using its own selector.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A single logs selector helps that one component, but every other selector-less call site still subscribes to the whole store, so a notification push re-renders the build list and settings panel; and without shallow equality, a selector returning a derived object re-renders on every unrelated update due to new object identity.

title: Introduce derived selector memoization with reselect to fix O(n^2) filtering in a 50k-row IoT table
business_context: An IoT fleet console renders a table of 50,000 sensors. The filtered+sorted+grouped view is recomputed inline in render on every state change, an O(n log n) pass that runs even when an unrelated panel updates. Typing one character in the search box recomputes the full pipeline 3x (search, filter, group are separate inline reduces), pegging a core and freezing input for ~250ms.
constraints: Filter/sort/group pipeline must memoize on its exact inputs
Search keystrokes must not recompute when filters/sort are unchanged
Must handle 50k rows under 16ms for incremental input
Grouping result must be stable-referenced so virtualized rows don't remount
Selectors must be unit-testable in isolation

before_state.description: The whole derive pipeline runs inline in render on every change with no memoization.

issues: Four full passes over 50k rows on every render
Runs even when an unrelated store field changes
group() returns a new array each time -> rows remount

reasoning: Naive approach: wrap each step in its own useMemo with the same broad deps. Rejected because listing sensors+search+filters+sort in each memo means any change busts all four. Alternative: move derivation to a Web Worker, rejected as premature; the real fix is memoizing the dependency chain so only the changed stage recomputes. Chosen: reselect createSelector composing searched->filtered->sorted->grouped, each selector keyed only on the inputs it actually uses, so a search keystroke only re-runs from searched downward and an unrelated update recomputes nothing. Trace: store change -> selectors recompute only stages whose inputs changed -> grouped result is referentially stable when inputs equal -> virtualized rows keep identity -> tests assert recompute counts per stage.

after_state.description: Composed reselect selectors memoize each pipeline stage on its real inputs, so only changed stages recompute.

files.purpose: Each stage memoizes on only the inputs it uses; unchanged stages return the prior referentially-stable result.
Binds the memoized grouped output to the component; identity is stable across unrelated updates.
Reads the memoized result, so an unrelated store change triggers zero pipeline work and rows keep identity.

migration_strategy: Add selectors behind FLEET_SELECTORS flag; render in parallel and assert output deep-equals the inline pipeline
Migrate SensorTable to useFleetView; profile recompute counts while typing in search
Verify an unrelated store update recomputes none of the four stages in canary
Ramp to all users; confirm input-to-paint latency drops below 16ms on a 50k fixture
Remove the inline pipeline and add a lint rule banning .filter().sort() chains in render
testing_strategy: Fixture: 50,000 sensors; instrument each selector with a call counter. Type one char into search and assert selSearched/selFiltered/selSorted/selGrouped each ran once (output: counts) while a subsequent unrelated store update increments none of them (side-effect). Assert selGrouped returns the same array reference when inputs are unchanged (referential stability). Chaos test: drive 1,000 random store updates touching only unrelated fields and assert zero pipeline recomputes; then change sort once and assert only selSorted+selGrouped recompute.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Wrapping each stage in useMemo with broad deps means every memo lists sensors/search/filters/sort, so a single keystroke busts all four memos and re-runs the full O(n) pipeline four times — identical cost to no memoization at all.

title: Replace context-based theme toggle that triggers full app re-render with CSS variables + a tiny store
business_context: A content platform's dark-mode toggle lives in ThemeContext consumed by 400+ components for color values. Toggling theme re-renders the entire app (a 6,000-node article reader), taking 900ms and causing a visible white flash. Product wants instant theme switching for an upcoming accessibility launch with a hard SLA of <50ms perceived switch.
constraints: Theme switch must be under 50ms with no full re-render
400+ components must keep reading semantic colors without prop changes
Must support system-preference and per-user override, persisted
SSR must render the correct theme to avoid hydration flash
Adding a new semantic color must not require editing every consumer

before_state.description: Every component reads color values from ThemeContext, so a toggle re-renders all 400+ consumers.

issues: colors object changes on toggle -> all 400 consumers re-render
6,000-node reader re-renders on theme switch
Adding a color means touching every consumer's inline style

reasoning: Naive approach: memoize colors and components. Rejected because the colors object identity changes on toggle by design, so memoization can't prevent the cascade. Alternative: a CSS-in-JS theme provider (styled-components ThemeProvider), rejected because it still re-renders styled components on theme change and the team wants zero React work for a switch. Chosen: define semantic colors as CSS custom properties on :root, toggle a data-theme attribute on <html>, and reduce React state to a tiny store holding only the mode (for the toggle UI and persistence). Components read var(--color-text); switching theme just flips an attribute — the browser repaints with no React re-render. Trace: toggle -> store.setMode -> set document attribute + persist -> CSS variables cascade -> zero component re-renders -> SSR sets the attribute server-side -> tests assert no re-render on toggle.

after_state.description: Semantic CSS variables flipped by a data-theme attribute; React state holds only the mode for the toggle.

files.purpose: Semantic tokens as CSS variables; a theme switch is a pure browser repaint with no React work.
Tiny store flips the html attribute and persists; only the toggle UI subscribes, not 400 components.
Only the toggle button subscribes to mode; color-consuming components read CSS variables and never re-render.

migration_strategy: Add CSS variables and themeStore behind CSS_THEME flag; keep ThemeContext for color reads initially
Migrate the article reader to var() colors and measure switch time in canary
Set the data-theme attribute in the SSR document head to prevent hydration flash
Ramp to all users; confirm switch under 50ms with zero React re-renders via profiler
Remove ThemeContext color reads after all components use CSS variables
testing_strategy: Fixture: a 6,000-node reader rendered with var()-based styles. Toggle theme via the store and assert no component re-rendered (side-effect via global render counter) and document.documentElement.dataset.theme flipped (output). Assert localStorage persisted the new mode. Negative test: server-render with data-theme='dark' and assert the client hydrates without a theme mismatch warning; chaos test: toggle 500 times rapidly and assert React render count stays 0 for color consumers while the attribute and persistence stay consistent.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Memoizing the colors object can't help because a toggle intentionally swaps light for dark colors, changing the object identity that every one of the 400 consumers depends on — React must re-render all of them, and the 6,000-node reader repaints through React instead of via a single CSS cascade.

title: Convert imperative websocket-to-setState chat into an external store with optimistic message send
business_context: A customer-support chat keeps messages in component useState appended on websocket events. Sending a message awaits the server ack before showing it (~500ms), so agents type ahead and lose track of what sent. Reconnects replay the message history and duplicate every message because there's no id-based dedupe, doubling transcripts for ~5% of sessions.
constraints: Sent messages must appear instantly (optimistic) with a pending indicator
Server ack must reconcile the optimistic message by client temp id
Reconnect replay must dedupe by message id (no duplicates)
Multiple chat windows for the same conversation must stay in sync
Out-of-order delivery must sort by server sequence, not arrival

before_state.description: Messages live in useState appended on socket events; sends await ack and reconnects duplicate.

issues: Send awaits ack -> 500ms before the message appears
No id dedupe -> reconnect replays duplicate every message
Per-component state -> two windows of same convo diverge

reasoning: Naive approach: optimistically append then reconcile, keeping useState. Rejected because per-component state still diverges across windows and reconnect dedupe is awkward in a render closure. Alternative: React Query with manual cache writes on socket events, rejected because real-time append semantics and temp-id reconciliation fit a purpose-built store better than a request cache. Chosen: a conversationStore keyed by convId holding a Map by message id, supporting optimistic insert with a temp id, ack reconciliation that swaps temp->server id, and id-based dedupe on replay; windows read via useSyncExternalStore. Trace: send -> store.optimisticInsert(tempId) -> socket ack -> store.reconcile(tempId, server) -> socket replay -> store.upsertById dedupes -> all windows read shared sorted snapshot -> tests assert no duplicates and instant display.

after_state.description: A per-conversation external store with optimistic insert, ack reconciliation, and id dedupe shared across windows.

files.purpose: Map-by-id store with optimistic insert, temp->server reconcile, and id dedupe; snapshot sorts by server seq.
Optimistically inserts on send and reconciles on ack; every window for convId shares this snapshot.
Routes incoming and replayed messages through id-based upsert so reconnects never duplicate.

migration_strategy: Add conversationStore + useConversation behind CHAT_STORE flag; keep useState path live
Migrate one support queue; verify sent messages appear instantly with a pending dot in canary
Wire reconnect replay through upsertById and assert no duplicate transcripts in shadow logs
Ramp to all agents; open two windows on one convo and confirm they stay in sync
Remove the useState message path after duplicate-message rate hits zero
testing_strategy: Fixture: a stubbed socket that acks after 500ms and replays 10 prior messages on reconnect. Call send and assert the message appears synchronously with pending=true (output) before the ack; after ack assert the temp id is gone and the server id present (side-effect on the map). Trigger reconnect replay of already-present messages and assert the snapshot length is unchanged (dedupe). Chaos test: deliver 200 messages with shuffled seq and a 10% duplicate rate and assert the snapshot is sorted, deduped, and identical across two subscribed windows.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Optimistic append on top of per-component useState still keeps separate arrays per chat window, so a second window doesn't see the optimistic message; and appending socket events without id dedupe means a reconnect replay re-pushes the full history, doubling the transcript.

title: Refactor a multi-step payment reducer to guard against double-charge via an idempotency state machine
business_context: A fintech checkout's payment flow uses booleans (submitting, succeeded) to gate the Pay button. A slow 3DS challenge let users click Pay twice before submitting flipped, causing 38 double-charges last quarter (each a manual refund + chargeback risk). The retry-after-failure path also re-submits with a fresh idempotency key, so a network blip that actually succeeded server-side charges twice.
constraints: Pay can be initiated at most once per idempotency key; the key must be stable across retries
State must move through idle->authorizing->challenged->capturing->done|failed deterministically
A retry must reuse the same idempotency key, never mint a new one
3DS challenge resumption must restore the in-flight key after redirect
No client state may permit a second charge while one is in flight or already succeeded

before_state.description: Boolean gates plus per-attempt idempotency keys allow a second click and retry-time key regeneration to double-charge.

issues: Second click before setSubmitting commits double-fires pay()
New idempotency key per attempt defeats server idempotency on retry
3DS redirect loses the in-flight key

reasoning: Naive approach: add a useRef lock around pay(). Rejected because it still mints a new key on retry, so a succeeded-but-timed-out charge gets re-attempted with a key the server hasn't seen, charging twice. Alternative: rely purely on server idempotency, rejected because without a stable client key the server can't dedupe distinct attempts. Chosen: a payment reducer with an explicit state machine and a single idempotency key minted once per intent; transitions forbid re-authorizing from authorizing/capturing/done, and the key plus state persist to survive the 3DS redirect. Trace: Pay -> START mints key once -> AUTHORIZE (no re-entry allowed) -> CHALLENGE persists key -> resume restores key -> CAPTURE -> done/failed -> retry reuses the key via RETRY action -> tests assert single charge under double-click and retry.

after_state.description: A deterministic payment state machine with one persistent idempotency key per intent; retries reuse the key.

files.purpose: State machine where START is ignored once authorizing and RETRY reuses the same key, making a second charge unrepresentable.
Persists the machine (including the idempotency key) so a 3DS redirect resumes the same in-flight charge.
Drives the machine; a second click while authorizing is a no-op and retries reuse the persisted key.

migration_strategy: Land payMachine + usePayment behind PAY_FSM flag; mirror boolean state to it read-only first
Switch a low-volume payment method to the machine; verify single charge under simulated double-click in canary
Persist the key and validate 3DS resume reuses it across the redirect in a staging gateway
Ramp by payment method, watching double-charge and duplicate-refund metrics
Remove boolean gates after a full quarter with zero double-charges
testing_strategy: Fixture: drive START then a second START within the same authorizing state. Assert the second START is ignored (output: state.tag still authorizing) and that charge() was invoked exactly once with one key (side-effect via spy). Simulate FAIL then RETRY and assert the same key is reused. Chaos test: replay START->CHALLENGE->RESUME->CAPTURED with a simulated redirect (reload from sessionStorage mid-flow) and assert exactly one capture occurs; fuzz 1,000 random event sequences and assert the machine never reaches done via two distinct keys.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A useRef lock prevents the second click but does nothing about the retry path, which mints a fresh idempotency key — so when a charge actually succeeded but the response timed out, the retry presents a new key the server treats as a separate charge, double-billing the customer.

title: Decouple a notification center store from the global app context to allow background toast batching
business_context: A B2B SaaS shows toasts from a global AppContext array. During a bulk import, the backend emits 200 progress events in 4 seconds; each pushes to the context array, re-rendering the whole shell 200 times and stacking 200 toasts that overflow the viewport. The toast array also lives in the same context as the sidebar collapse state, so collapsing the sidebar wipes pending toasts on some code paths.
constraints: Toast pushes must not re-render the app shell; only the toast container re-renders
Rapid bursts must batch/coalesce (group same-type progress toasts)
Toast state must be isolated from unrelated UI state (sidebar, modals)
Max N visible toasts with a queue for overflow
Auto-dismiss timers must survive component re-mounts

before_state.description: Toasts share the global context array; pushes re-render the shell and mix with unrelated UI state.

issues: Each toast push re-renders the entire shell
Toasts coupled with sidebar state -> accidental wipes
No coalescing -> 200 toasts overflow the screen

reasoning: Naive approach: throttle setToasts. Rejected because throttling drops progress updates and toasts still live in the shell-wide context. Alternative: a third-party toast lib, rejected because the design system mandates custom toasts and we need server-driven coalescing rules. Chosen: an isolated toastStore (outside React) with a bounded visible set, a queue for overflow, and coalescing by a groupKey so 200 progress events collapse into one updating toast; the container subscribes via useSyncExternalStore. Timers live in the store, surviving re-mounts. Trace: event -> store.push (coalesce by groupKey) -> only toast container re-renders -> queue drains as toasts dismiss -> timers in store -> shell untouched -> tests assert shell render count flat and coalescing.

after_state.description: An isolated toast store with coalescing, a bounded visible set, an overflow queue, and store-held timers.

files.purpose: Coalesces by groupKey, bounds visible toasts with an overflow queue, and owns dismiss timers across re-mounts.
Only the toast container subscribes; pushing a toast never touches the app shell.
Renders only the bounded visible set; a 200-event burst coalesces into one updating progress toast.

migration_strategy: Add toastStore and ToastContainer behind TOAST_STORE flag; keep context toasts live
Route bulk-import progress through groupKey coalescing in canary and confirm one toast updates
Verify the app shell does not re-render on toast pushes via profiler
Ramp to all tenants; check viewport overflow incidents drop to zero
Remove toasts from AppContext and delete the coupled sidebar wipe path
testing_strategy: Fixture: fire 200 progress events sharing groupKey='import' in 4s with fake timers.
